data.title: Trump discusses potential significant change to Affordable Care Act
data.description:
President Donald Trump has finally hinted at one of the key changes he would like to see made to the Affordable Care Act, one of the signature legacies of former President Barack Obama that guarantees health insurance coverage for all Americans.

In a Fox News interview, Trump said that he would end the subsidies that are currently used to defray premium costs for lower-income Americans and that go to insurers. "I want the money to go into an account for people where the people buy their own health insurance," he told Laura Ingrham, adding it would lead to better insurance and lower costs for consumers. 

"They're going to feel like entrepreneurs," Trump added. "They're actually able to go out and negotiate their own health insurance."

The president also commented that the revamped health insurance program could be named after him. "Call it Trumpcare. Call it whatever you want to call it. Anything but Obamacare."

Trump was criticized in September 2024 following a debate against former Vice President and Democratic nominee Kamala Harris in which he responded he has "concepts of a plan" in response to a question on what he would do to reform Obamacare. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Cboe the latest exchange to enter prediction markets arena - report
data.description:
Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) is looking to roll out its own prediction markets offering in the coming months, according to a Thursday media report, making it the latest exchange to enter the fast-growing prediction markets space.

Cboe (CBOE) is specifically planning to introduce products linked to economic events and financial outcomes, CEO Craig Donohue told Bloomberg in an interview.

Fellow derivatives exchange operator CME Group (CME) said earlier in the week it will power a prediction market platform with FanDuel (FLUT). Retail trading app Robinhood Markets (HOOD) entered the prediction markets late last year, when it allowed customers to trade event contracts on the results of the 2024 U.S. presidential election.

Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the owner of the New York Stock Exchange, recently agreed to invest up to $2B in prediction marketplace Polymarket (POLYMARKET).

data.title: Boeing defense workers approve new contract, ending 100-day strike
data.description:
Boeing (BA) defense workers approved the company's latest contract offer, union officials said Thursday, ending a more than three-month strike that has disrupted production of F-15 fighter jets and other programs.

The ~3,200 members of the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers' District 837 have pushed Boeing (BA) for a higher ratification bonus and improved retirement plan contributions; the company revised its offer earlier this week, reducing the overall bonus but increasing the upfront cash incentive to $6,000 from $3,000 in the previous proposal.

Boeing's (BA) defense unit accounted for ~30% of the company's $65.5 billion in revenues amassed during the first nine months of 2025.

The union said the workers will begin returning to Boeing (BA) factories on November 16.

data.title: Ardent Health outlines $40M annual benefit from impact program as cost headwinds persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ardent Health, Inc. (ARDT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Martin Bonick opened by highlighting "a continuation of growth momentum we've experienced across our business, driven by robust demand, improving surgical trends and disciplined execution," noting year-to-date adjusted EBITDA is up 30% and margin improvements. He acknowledged that "our earnings performance this quarter did not meet our expectations" and announced a revision to full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance to $530 million to $555 million, attributing this to "persistent industry-wide cost pressures, particularly those around professional fees and payer denials that have proven more durable than anticipated."
  • Bonick stated, "These pressures are not demand-driven, and our revenue guidance remains unchanged, but our earnings pull-through has been impacted, and we are taking decisive actions to address it."
  • He emphasized the company's "impact program" and upcoming cost optimization initiatives, with actions expected to contribute in Q4 and ramp through 2026. Bonick added, "Our strong platform, combined with initiatives to improve capacity and efficiency drove admissions growth of 5.8% in the quarter."
  • CFO Alfred Lumsdaine reported, "Third quarter revenue increased 8.8% to $1.58 billion compared to the prior year, driven by adjusted admissions growth of 2.9% and net patient service revenue per adjusted admission growth of 5.8%." He further explained, "Adjusted EBITDA increased 46% in the third quarter to $143 million compared to the prior year, and adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 240 basis points to 9.1%."
  • Lumsdaine detailed two nonrecurring items: a $43 million revenue reduction due to a change in accounting estimate from transitioning to the Kodiak RCA net revenue platform, and a $54 million increase in professional and general liability reserves fully attributable to the New Mexico market, both excluded from adjusted EBITDA.

Outlook

  • The company revised its 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to $530 million to $555 million, with Lumsdaine noting, "At the midpoint implies growth of 9% and 20 basis points of margin expansion. However, we're maintaining our previous revenue guidance of $6.2 billion to $6.45 billion or 6% growth at the midpoint."
  • Bonick reiterated, "Our updated outlook prudently assumes these industry headwinds observed in the third quarter will persist at elevated levels in the fourth quarter."
  • Management expects margin enhancement and efficiency initiatives to provide an annual benefit of more than $40 million as these actions reach full run rate in early 2026.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the third quarter was reported at $1.58 billion, reflecting 8.8% growth year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA reached $143 million, a 46% increase, with a margin of 9.1%.
  • Lumsdaine noted, "Year-to-date through the third quarter, adjusted EBITDA grew 30% and margins expanded 150 basis points to 8.7% compared to the prior year."
  • Cash flow from operating activities was $154 million for the third quarter, with total cash of $609 million and total available liquidity of $904 million at quarter-end.
  • Third quarter results included an estimated $15 million to $20 million of earnings initially expected in Q4, affecting the timing of reported adjusted EBITDA.
  • The company ended the quarter with total debt outstanding of $1.1 billion, net leverage of 1.0x, and lease adjusted net leverage of 2.5x.

Q&A

  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim: Asked about headwinds and tailwinds for 2026 and expectations for EBITDA growth. Bonick responded, "We're going to wait until our fourth quarter call in February to provide that '26 guidance," but emphasized "strong durable demand as we go into next year" and ongoing execution of the impact program.
  • Cassorla followed up on share repurchase considerations. Lumsdaine replied, "It would be premature. We wouldn't want to speak to the Board, but...over time, I'm confident that the Board will look at every option to optimize shareholder value."
  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink: Queried the impact of malpractice developments and the revenue cycle change. Lumsdaine clarified, "100% of that charge relates to the New Mexico market where we have seen significant social inflationary pressure in medical malpractice cases." Regarding the revenue cycle, he said, "This is a change in accounting estimate...the difference is reserve timing between the 2 models, and it results in a reduction in net revenue just upon implementation."
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the scope of payer denials and future expectations. Bonick explained the denials are "fairly uniform across all of those different categories" and described steps including "ramping up our litigation efforts and demand letters."
  • Kevin Fischbeck, BofA: Inquired about the IMPACT program and margin opportunities. Bonick highlighted, "We're growing that outpatient platform. And through our transfer centers, we've seen robust inpatient growth better than most of our peers."
  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC: Sought clarification on contract renegotiations to address denial activity. Lumsdaine said, "We termed it. Payer came back to the table. We negotiated a better rate and better terms to prevent the denial activity that we were seeing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a neutral to slightly negative tone, focusing on headwinds from payer denials, professional fees, and the sustainability of margin expansion. Several questions pressed for details on contract renegotiations and the impact program’s efficacy.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in prepared remarks, repeatedly stressing "decisive actions," "strong demand," and visibility on "margin enhancement initiatives." During Q&A, responses were measured, with phrases like "it's a little bit too early" and "we think it's prudent to continue to expect this level of denials for the immediate future," indicating caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment was more cautious, reflecting increased cost headwinds and the need to recalibrate guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a downward revision of adjusted EBITDA guidance, contrasting with reaffirmed guidance in the prior quarter.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to immediate cost containment and margin recovery, moving from the prior quarter's emphasis on expansion, innovation, and regulatory navigation.
  • Analysts’ questions pivoted from growth and regulatory mitigation to the durability of cost pressures and the effectiveness of impact initiatives.
  • Management’s tone shifted from optimistic growth to a more defensive, pragmatic stance, explicitly acknowledging that earnings “did not meet our expectations.”
  • Key metrics revealed a slowdown in EBITDA growth rate and increased attention to nonrecurring charges impacting the quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistent professional fee increases, particularly in radiology, anesthesia, and ER, remain a challenge, with fees rising 11% in the third quarter.
  • Payer denials escalated, notably across managed care, Medicare, Medicaid, and health exchanges, resulting in further revenue headwinds.
  • The company recorded a $54 million reserve increase due to adverse medical malpractice claims in New Mexico, reflecting "significant social inflationary pressure."
  • Management is focusing on renegotiating contracts, reducing workforce, tightening agency labor contracts, and implementing vendor consolidation to address these risks.

Final Takeaway

Ardent Health management underscored that, despite robust demand and strong volume growth, persistent cost pressures from professional fees and payer denials have necessitated a revision to earnings guidance. The company is actively implementing targeted cost and margin initiatives expected to yield over $40 million in annual benefits, while maintaining a disciplined approach to growth investments. Management remains confident in the underlying demand environment, the effectiveness of its impact program, and its ability to deliver sustainable margin expansion and adjusted EBITDA growth over the longer term.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Market Voices: Verizon's turnaround plan, foreclosures, jobs report
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks.

  • Verizon (VZ) Chairman Mark Bertolini said that newly appointed CEO Dan Schulman is working on a plan to turn the company around amid declining market share.

“Verizon has gone from number one in market cap, bond ratings and market share to number three. And the network isn’t as differentiated as it used to be, in large part because everybody’s been spending money to put these 5G networks in place,” Bertolini told CNBC. “So losing 30% share over the last eight years is an issue, and we have to do something different.”

Bertolini noted that Schulman was currently reviewing the company, including its underlying cost structures, and plans to release a turnaround plan "sooner rather than later."

“We believe that once we have that plan in place, we’ll have a good story,” Bertolini added. “The Street reacted early on that there’s going to be a price war; I think it’s less about price war than the value of what we’re offering to people through the product.”

  • Foreclosure filings shot up 19% year-over-year in October, according to property data provider ATTOM.

The firm also said in its October report that filings such as default notices and bank repossessions were up 3% from September, marking eight straight months of increases year-over-year, according to CNBC. 

  • National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said the government's October jobs report will not contain unemployment rate data due to the shutdown. 

“The household survey wasn’t conducted in October, so we’re going to get half the employment report,” Hassett told Fox News, according to Bloomberg. “We’ll get the jobs part, but we won’t get the unemployment rate, and that’ll just be for one month.”

The October jobs report had been slated to be released on Nov. 7.

data.title: Roche’s breast cancer drug Perjeta faces copycats as FDA approves first biosimilar
data.description:
• The U.S. Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) on Thursday approved Poherdy, developed by Shanghai Henlius Biotech (OTCPK:SGBCF), as the first biosimilar referencing Perjeta, a blockbuster breast cancer therapy from Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY).
  • The FDA said it approved Poherdy as an interchangeable biosimilar, meaning the drug can be substituted at the pharmacy level without the intervention of a healthcare provider who first prescribed the reference product.
  • Poherdy will be approved in the U.S. for breast cancer indications, including HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer and HER2-positive early breast cancer in combination with established oncology drugs trastuzumab and chemotherapy, the regulator said.
  • Perjeta generated CHF 2.32B ($2.92B) in sales for the Swiss drugmaker in the first nine months of this year, implying an ~13% YoY decline amid rising uptake for Phesgo, which combines the monoclonal antibody with trastuzumab into a novel single injection.

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as post-shutdown optimism fades, rate cut odds fall below 50%
data.description:
Wall Street's major indexes were significantly lower on Thursday as heightened market sentiment due to the end of the U.S. government shutdown dissipated, and focus was turned to the economy.

Odds of a December 25-basis-point interest rate cut have fallen to 47.6%, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.6% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -2.5%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.3%.

Some of the mega-caps leading the decline within the S&P 500 ((SP500)) include Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Tesla (TSLA), and Palantir (PLTR).

The U.S. House of Representatives on Wednesday passed the stopgap spending bill that will end the longest U.S. government shutdown in history, which lasted 43 days. With it, President Donald Trump signed into law a federal spending package.

With the shutdown now over for the time being, investors are hoping that delayed economic reports will soon start to come. However, two major economic reports on jobs and inflation for October may never be released even as the government shutdown ends, according to White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt.

“The gears of the government should be working again soon, and while that is a relief for markets and the economy, there is still plenty of uncertainty, particularly around the missed inflation and jobs data and how these fronts have been faring. While we have always expected that many of the data points missed during the shutdown will remain dark, there are questions about what the inflation and jobs data will look like once these reports come back online,” said Carol Schleif, chief market strategist at BMO Private Wealth.

“We would not be surprised to see some market chop over the coming weeks as the government gears and economic data presses get turning again. The data blackout has made the Federal Reserve's job difficult, but we still expect them to cut interest rates again in December,” Schleif added.

In addition, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said on Thursday that the Fed should remain “somewhat restrictive” due to softening in the labor market, and inflation still above the Fed's 2% target.

On the earnings side, The Walt Disney Company (DIS) was last -7.7% after the company published its FQ4 earnings report. Revenue dipped 0.5% year-over-year as the Entertainment segment saw revenue decline 6%.

On the other hand, Cisco (CSCO) shares were +4.6% after the networking equipment giant reported fiscal first-quarter results that were above Wall Street's estimates and raised its full-year forecast. The company's stock is near its all-time high.

“Earnings so far have been better than expected, with double-digit growth rates and solid participation across most economic sectors. Better yet, the bottom line is coming in well ahead of strong revenue growth, indicating intense focus on extracting margin improvement,” said Schleif.

“It's becoming clear that earnings expectations have been too low ever since they were cut in the aftermath of Liberation Day in April. That has also helped stocks clear a lower bar and helped to propel stocks to record heights,” she added.

On crypto, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) dropped below $100,000 for the first time since May.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.11%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.59%. A $25B, 30-Year Bond Auction tailed.

The Fed's balance sheet will land later in the day.

Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Wednesday, with the Dow Jones reaching a new record high close, while Nasdaq ended in the red, as several big tech stocks lagged.

More on the markets:

data.title: Fmr. Minneapolis Fed president: It is ‘difficult to build rationale’ for lowering rates
data.description:
Former Minneapolis Fed President Gary Stern believes there’s “no compelling rationale to lower rates right away.”

Stern’s cautious approach aligns with recent statements from Boston Fed President Collins, suggesting a preference for slowing down the pace of monetary policy adjustments despite previous rate cuts.

During a CNBC interview, the veteran central banker emphasized the current economic conditions don’t warrant immediate action, citing stubborn inflation “in the 2.5-3% plus range” as a key concern.

“Given where the economy is, the way it’s performing, and the stubbornness of inflation… it seems to me it’s difficult to build a rationale, to build a case for lowering rates at this point,” he said.

Stern highlighted that employment gains continue but remain modest, while consumer spending has generally held up well.

He expressed skepticism about dramatic changes in economic conditions in the near term, noting: “I’d be surprised if in the next month or two or three, we got a very different picture from the data.”

The former Fed official cautioned against overreacting to individual data points, emphasizing the inherently “noisy” nature of economic indicators.

“It’s always a good idea to not only do the analysis, but to smooth out the readings you get month after month and see if the trajectory of the economy is changing,” he said.

Regarding the labor market, Stern stressed the importance of understanding its dynamic nature rather than focusing on isolated corporate announcements.

“We tend to have a very dynamic labor market month after month after month… if the net increase in employment in a month is, say, 50,000, underpinning that is probably a million people who either quit their jobs or lost their jobs and a million plus 50,000 who were hired,” he explained, warning against emphasizing “any one report coming out of whatever company you may be following.”

Odds of a December 25-basis-point interest rate cut have fallen to 47.6%, according to the CME FedWatch tool, while odds of no change are at 52.4%.

More on U.S. economy:

  • Fed policy needs to stay 'somewhat restrictive', Cleveland Fed's Hammack says
  • Mortgage rates essentially flat
  • October jobs report won't have unemployment rate, Hassett says
  • Jobs, CPI reports for October may never be released - White House

data.title: Pure Cycle outlines 2026 recurring revenue growth and single-family rental acceleration as housing headwinds persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pure Cycle Corporation (PCYO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Harding, President and CEO, emphasized, "continued profitability where you've got 25 straight quarters of profitability and this quarter and this year is no different." He highlighted "continued growth in each of the revenue segments, especially in the recurring revenue segments," with stable earnings across water, wastewater, land development, and rental income. Harding noted, "Q4 is typically our strongest quarter," but revenue was "slightly down, mostly due to the housing headwinds and pushing some of that revenue recognition from our percent completion into Q1 2026."
  • Harding reported that "net income and earnings per share... actually exceeded our expectations" due to oil and gas royalty income coming in stronger than projected, citing "the completion of an additional 6 or 7 wells into the largest portion of our royalty estate" as a key driver.
  • The water utility segment was described as having "a little over 1,600 commercial connection points... out of a total of 60,000 potential," with recurring revenues paced on this 60,000 connection guidance. Tap fees were noted at "right around that $40,000 mark."
  • The land development segment delivered "228 lots of Phase 2C that we had forecasted for 2025," with deferred revenue of about $800,000 spilling into Q1 2026 due to regulatory delays. Harding stated that "overall, sales in Land Development were off then from our expectations, and that was largely attributable to some of the headwinds that we're seeing in housing."
  • The single-family rental segment experienced delays from regulatory updates but has "about 40 homes under contract now that are delivering from several of our homebuilders," with expectations for a "substantial increase in the number of single-family rentals in 2026 and into 2027."
  • Harding mentioned a "continued strong balance sheet," with investment focus on asset growth, liquidity, and share repurchases.
  • CFO Marc Spezialy did not provide a separate prepared statement in the transcript.

Outlook

  • Harding projected that in 2026, recurring revenue is expected to continue growing, "not only from our water customers, but also some of our single-family rental," and forecasted a build-out target of "as many as 250 to 300 units" for single-family rentals. He outlined a short-term objective to reach "up to about 100 units" in the rental segment and described the land development segment as moving toward "about 30%" completion of Sky Ranch in the next period.
  • Management signaled a "range of earnings per share" for 2026, with variance tied to the sensitivity of lot deliveries and industrial water sales, but did not disclose specific guidance figures in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • Harding stated, "revenue for Q4, again, it was our highest quarter, slightly down, mostly due to the housing headwinds and pushing some of that revenue recognition from our percent completion into Q1 2026."
  • Despite revenue and gross profit being "slightly off from what we saw in 2024," net income and EPS "exceeded our expectations," attributed to stronger oil and gas royalty income.
  • The company reported "forecasted net income was right around $12.5 million, slightly lower revenue from our land development segment," with higher costs of revenue due to tariffs and inflation, but offset by lower G&A expenses and higher oil and gas royalties.
  • On the water segment, annual revenues are described as "about $1,600 per connection per year," with the recurring customer base growing at a "22% CAGR."
  • The land development segment had deferred revenues and regulatory delays impacting timing, with some revenue shifting into the next fiscal year.

Q&A

  • Greg Vennett, a shareholder, asked, "Has housing sales in your areas slowed down due to affordability or... are the builders still building?" Harding responded that "Denver is probably on the high side of unaffordability," but Pure Cycle's entry-level segment remains resilient, stating "we are in that affordable market segment... probably in that 4% of homes that are delivered on an annual basis are in that affordable price segment."
  • An unnamed analyst inquired about land acquisitions and commercial development. Harding replied, "we really do have our nets out, and we're saving a bit of liquidity just for that," adding that conversations around acquisitions are strengthening, but timing is critical. On commercial, he noted "we are seeing interest from a lot of those" and that growth projected in 2028 is "mostly a factor of being able to open up Phase 3 with the interchange."
  • The analyst added, "the near-term weakness that you're seeing from some of the homebuilders may be a long-term benefit for you guys in that the land may not be as expensive." Harding acknowledged, "we're very disciplined in what we pay" and that some sellers may be motivated by timing rather than price.
  • Vennett also asked about competition in water provision and commercial development. Harding stated, "we're the best provider, but we're not the only," citing City of Aurora as main competition and describing Pure Cycle's advantages in location and cost structure.
  • Matt Reiner, Aranda, asked about lower 2026 earnings forecast despite higher revenues. Harding explained, "2025 had a high profitability because of kind of the oil and gas, and that's almost 100% margin...we're not likely to see the same earnings per share bump that we saw in 2025 because of that profitability of the oil and gas royalties."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed slightly negative sentiment, focusing on housing headwinds, acquisition timing, and competitive advantages. Questions pressed for detail on margin differences, cost structure, and the impact of market softness.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing flexibility and recurring growth, stating "the resiliency of our builders and our project is strengthened by the fact that we are in that affordable market segment" and "we're very disciplined in what we pay."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed a more cautious tone, with increased attention to headwinds and regulatory delays, but management continued to stress business model flexibility and multiple revenue streams.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management address slightly lower revenue and gross profit due to housing headwinds and regulatory delays, while net income and EPS were boosted by oil and gas royalty outperformance.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize recurring revenue growth and single-family rental acceleration, with a cautious approach to land development and acquisitions.
  • Analyst questions this quarter probed deeper into acquisition opportunities, commercial development timing, and margin sustainability, compared to the previous quarter's focus on operational progress and water asset development.
  • Key metric changes included shifting revenue recognition into the next fiscal year and a notable increase in single-family rental targets.
  • Management's confidence remained steady, but the tone reflected greater market caution and focus on operational agility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted housing market headwinds and regulatory delays as key challenges, with some land development revenue deferred into the next year.
  • Cost inflation and tariffs were noted as contributing to higher costs of revenue, though mitigated by controlling SG&A expenses and diversifying revenue streams.
  • Analysts expressed concern about acquisition timing, competitive landscape in water provision, and the sustainability of high-margin oil and gas royalties.

Final Takeaway

Pure Cycle Corporation reported a solid quarter of profitability, with earnings per share slightly exceeding expectations due to strong oil and gas royalties, even as revenue and gross profit were impacted by housing market headwinds and regulatory delays. Management signaled continued growth in recurring revenue segments, an accelerated expansion of single-family rentals, and a disciplined approach to land and commercial development acquisitions. The outlook emphasizes operational flexibility and capital discipline as the company maneuvers through housing affordability challenges and positions itself for long-term asset growth and monetization across its core business segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners on track to extend gains for the seventh straight trading day
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is on track to close in green for the seventh consecutive trading session, with the stock trading 1.50% higher at $32.05 during Thursday afternoon.

The Houston, Texas-based midstream energy services provider has added 4.40% over the course of the last six trading days.

A notable gain was logged on Wednesday, November 5. The stock closed the day 1.49% higher at $30.67. Enterprise Products Partners filed its quarterly report with the Securities and Exchange Commission on the day.

The company reported mixed financial results on October 30, with the Q3 GAAP EPS missing the Wall Street analysts' estimates and revenue surpassing consensus.

Notably, Enterprise Products reported an increase in its Q3 NGL, crude, refined products, and petrochemical transportation volumes.

"While EPD is suffering from some project delays and is currently in a zone where leverage is rising a bit due to having to lean into spending on growth investments, the future looks very bright for the company," said Seeking Alpha analyst Samuel Smith.

"It is rapidly approaching its cash flow inflection point and appears poised to return a considerable amount of cash flow to unit holders while also seeing its leverage ratio drop significantly," added Smith.

On average, SA authors and the Wall Street analysts grade the stock as Buy.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha Quant Rating system gives EPD a Hold rating, with a score of 3.04 on a scale of 5. The stock is rated C+ for Valuation; C- for Growth, Momentum, and Revisions; and A for Profitability.
